topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery air felt thick, heavy with the scent of turpentine and expensive varnish. Elara traced the line of the crimson ink on the canvas, her breath catching in her throat. It was a masterpiece, undeniably, a vibrant explosion of emotion she’d poured into weeks of sleepless nights. Then, her gaze snagged on a detail near the corner—a small, almost hidden flourish that wasn't part of her original composition. It was a delicate, intricate pattern, undeniably a tattoo. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, pulling at her chest. She looked up, scanning the room, searching for the source of the intrusion. When she finally found the artist, Marcus, his face was pale, his eyes darting away from hers as if caught in some kind of spotlight. "That," Elara managed, her voice sounding brittle, "that detail... it's yours." Marcus flinched, a tiny, almost imperceptible movement, and the silence that followed felt vast and accusatory.

[story 2]

I stared at the photograph under the harsh fluorescent light. My hand trembled slightly as I brought it closer. The subject was my latest portrait, the one that had taken me three months to perfect, the one that felt so deeply, vulnerably mine. But there, etched onto the subject's wrist, was a stark, black design. It wasn't a stylistic choice; it was undeniably a tattoo, a mark of ownership. A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, leaving a dull ache behind my ribs. I wanted to crumple the paper, to erase the evidence of this unwelcome intrusion. When I finally turned to face the studio owner, my jaw was tight, my shoulders slumped as if the weight of the discovery had physically crushed me. "I... I didn't realize," I mumbled, the words barely audible, the shame clinging to my tongue like wet ash.

[story 3]

He was sitting across the drafting table, ostensibly discussing color palettes, but his eyes kept flicking towards the centerpiece—my most recent oil painting. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. I watched as he subtly shifted his posture, and then I saw it: a shadow of dark ink woven into the background of the figure I had painstakingly rendered. It was a deliberate alteration, a signature left in permanent skin. A profound sense of deflation settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I took a slow, measured breath, trying to keep my expression neutral, but my hands clenched into fists beneath the table. "That mark," I said, my tone flat, devoid of any real heat, "it belongs to you." The air between us suddenly seemed charged, thick with unspoken acknowledgment of a boundary crossed.
